records.name: Health and Social Care Act 2008 and Regulated Activities Regulations 2014 (CQC registration and fundamental standards)

citation.act: Health and Social Care Act 2008; The Health and Social Care Act 2008 (Regulated Activities) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/2936)
citation.section: HSCA 2008 s.10 (requirement to register); SI 2014/2936 Part 3 regs 4-20A (fundamental standards), reg 20 (duty of candour)

applies_when: carries on a regulated activity in England (personal care, treatment of disease/disorder/injury, surgical procedures, diagnostic and screening, family planning, maternity, etc.)
operates a care home, clinic, dental practice, GP surgery, hospital or similar in England
excluded_when: activity is not a CQC-regulated activity (Schedule 1 exemptions, e.g. some purely cosmetic non-surgical services)
provider operates only in Scotland, Wales or NI (different regulators: Care Inspectorate, HIW/CIW, RQIA)

website_obligations.duty: Be registered with the CQC for each regulated activity carried on and be findable on the CQC public register
elements: provider appears on the CQC register
registered regulated activities match the services advertised
registration certificate available on request

website_obligations.duty: Operate within the fundamental standards, including the statutory duty of candour (reg 20) for care and treatment
elements: safe care and treatment (reg 12)
duty of candour policy reflected in patient-facing complaints/openness information
complaints route published (reg 16)

penalty.basis: Carrying on a regulated activity without registration is a criminal offence (HSCA 2008 s.10, s.34) with an unlimited fine on conviction; fundamental-standards breaches causing avoidable harm (reg 12/13) carry unlimited fines; CQC may also cancel or suspend registration

intel.why_matters: Registration is the licence to operate in English healthcare; carrying on a regulated activity while unregistered is a criminal offence and instantly voids any trust the website tries to build.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is the provider registered with the CQC for the exact regulated activities its website advertises, and does the named registered manager match?
intel.relevance_hook: Patients and the CQC both check the register before believing a clinic; an unregistered or mismatched activity is the fastest route from marketing copy to prosecution.

records.name: Regulated Activities Regulations 2014 reg 20A (requirement to display CQC performance ratings)

citation.act: The Health and Social Care Act 2008 (Regulated Activities) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/2936)
citation.section: reg 20A (requirement as to display of performance assessments)

applies_when: provider has received a CQC rating (Outstanding, Good, Requires improvement or Inadequate)
provider maintains a website
excluded_when: provider has not yet been rated by the CQC (display 'not yet rated' where required)
activity is not one the CQC rates

website_obligations.duty: Display the most recent CQC rating on every website maintained by or on behalf of the provider, with a link to the CQC profile where the full assessment can be read
elements: current overall rating shown on the site
link to the provider's page on cqc.org.uk
rating updated within 21 days of CQC publication
'not yet rated' shown where no rating exists

website_obligations.duty: Display at least one rating sign at each premises from which regulated activities are provided
elements: rating displayed conspicuously at the premises

penalty.basis: Breach of reg 20A is a criminal offence; CQC typically issues a fixed penalty notice of GBP 100 for failure to display, and may prosecute directly without a prior warning notice, with a court fine up to level 4 on the standard scale (GBP 2,500) per offence, plus reputational enforcement

intel.why_matters: The CQC rating is the single trust signal patients look for, and reg 20A makes showing it on the website a legal duty CQC can prosecute directly without warning.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is the current CQC rating shown on the website with a working link to the CQC profile, and is it the latest rating?
intel.relevance_hook: A missing or stale rating badge is both a prosecutable breach and a conversion killer; it is the cheapest fix with the highest trust return on a healthcare site.

records.name: Human Medicines Regulations 2012 reg 284 (prohibition on advertising prescription only medicines to the public)

citation.section: reg 284 (advertisements likely to lead to the use of a prescription only medicine prohibited); reg 280 (prohibition of misleading advertising); Part 14 generally

applies_when: public-facing marketing names, pictures or alludes to a prescription only medicine (POM)
advertises botulinum toxin (Botox, Dysport, Bocouture, Azzalure, Xeomin), weight-loss injections (semaglutide/Wegovy, tirzepatide) or any other POM to the general public
excluded_when: advertisement is directed solely at persons qualified to prescribe or supply (healthcare professionals) via a gated channel
content refers only to a general consultation service without naming or depicting the POM

website_obligations.duty: Do not advertise any prescription only medicine to the public; remove product names, images, hashtags and indirect references (e.g. 'wrinkle-relaxing injections', 'fat jab') from public pages, ads and social
elements: no POM brand or generic name in public copy, images, alt text or hashtags
no indirect POM references that imply a named POM
consultation service may be offered without naming the POM

website_obligations.duty: For permitted general-sale/pharmacy medicine ads, include the mandatory particulars (name, indication, how to use safely, 'always read the label / ask your pharmacist')
elements: mandatory particulars present where an OTC medicine is advertised

penalty.basis: Advertising a POM to the public is a criminal offence under HMR 2012; on indictment up to two years' imprisonment and/or an unlimited fine, plus MHRA takedown and ASA enforcement; typical band reflects investigation/settlement exposure not the statutory ceiling

regulator.name: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA); Advertising Standards Authority (ASA/CAP) as backstop

enforcement.case: CAP/MHRA Enforcement Notice: advertising botulinum toxin injections on social media
enforcement.amount: Takedown / referral to MHRA and professional regulators
enforcement.summary: CAP, with the MHRA, warned the beauty and aesthetics sector that botulinum toxin is a POM that cannot be advertised to the public, including paid ads, organic posts, influencer content, indirect references and hashtags; monitoring technology used to flag and remove non-compliant ads.

intel.why_matters: Every aesthetics and online-prescribing site that names Botox or a weight-loss jab in public copy is committing a criminal advertising offence, and the ASA now finds these automatically.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does any public page, image, alt text or hashtag name or depict a prescription only medicine rather than only offering a consultation?
intel.relevance_hook: This is the highest-frequency healthcare breach we find: the POM name sits in a headline, an image caption or a hashtag, and an automated scan flags it before any human complains.

sources: seed compliance_laws.json UK-MHRA-HMR / UK-MHRA-POM-BOTOX (section range corrected from 286-297 to reg 284)

records.name: Botulinum Toxin and Cosmetic Fillers (Children) Act 2021 (offence of administering to under-18s)

citation.act: Botulinum Toxin and Cosmetic Fillers (Children) Act 2021 (c. 19)
citation.section: s.1 (offence of administering botulinum toxin or a filler for a cosmetic purpose to a person under 18 in England); s.2 (offence of making arrangements or booking)

applies_when: offers botulinum toxin or dermal/cosmetic filler injections for a cosmetic purpose in England
website could allow a person under 18 to book, or markets these treatments without an age restriction
excluded_when: treatment administered for a medical purpose under the direction of a registered medical practitioner (statutory exemption)
provider operates only outside England (Act applies to administration in England)
NEGATION GUARD: a statement that the clinic does NOT treat under-18s, or an age-verification gate, is COMPLIANT and must not be scored as a breach

website_obligations.duty: Do not offer, market, arrange or allow booking of botulinum toxin or cosmetic filler injections for anyone under 18; state the 18+ restriction and gate bookings by age
elements: explicit statement that treatments are 18+ only
booking flow captures/verifies age or blocks under-18s
no imagery or influencer content targeting under-18s

penalty.basis: Criminal offence; on summary conviction an unlimited fine in England; the person administering, and anyone booking or arranging the treatment for a minor, are both liable

intel.why_matters: Since October 2021 it is a crime to inject a minor with Botox or filler for cosmetic reasons, and taking the booking is itself an offence, so an ungated booking page is direct criminal exposure.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does the site prevent an under-18 from booking botulinum toxin or filler, and does it state the 18+ rule?
intel.relevance_hook: An open booking form with no age gate turns a marketing page into a chargeable offence the moment a 17-year-old books; the fix is a visible 18+ policy and an age check.

records.name: GMC Good Medical Practice 2024 (honest, non-misleading advertising by doctors)

citation.act: Medical Act 1983 (regulator's statutory basis); GMC Good Medical Practice 2024 (professional standards)
citation.section: Medical Act 1983 Part V (fitness to practise); Good Medical Practice 2024, esp. paras on honesty in communications and financial/commercial dealings

applies_when: a service is doctor-led or names GMC-registered doctors
website makes claims about a named doctor's qualifications, competence or results
excluded_when: no doctor named and no doctor-led claims made

website_obligations.duty: Ensure information about doctors and services is honest, accurate and not misleading, and display each doctor's GMC registration so it can be checked
elements: GMC reference number shown for named doctors
qualifications and specialties stated accurately and verifiable on the GMC register
no exaggerated or unsubstantiated outcome or superiority claims
no exploitative product endorsement

penalty.basis: Non-statutory professional standard enforced by the GMC via fitness-to-practise proceedings under the Medical Act 1983; sanctions include warnings, conditions, suspension or erasure rather than fines

intel.why_matters: A doctor's registration and honesty of advertising are the trust anchors of a private clinic; overstated credentials or results are a fitness-to-practise risk, not just a marketing choice.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is each named doctor's GMC number shown and do the stated qualifications match the GMC register?
intel.relevance_hook: Buyers verify a doctor on the GMC register in seconds; a missing GMC number or an inflated title reads as a red flag to patients and the regulator alike.

records.name: GDC Standards for the Dental Team and ethical advertising guidance (dental practices)

citation.act: Dentists Act 1984 (regulator's statutory basis); GDC Standards for the Dental Team; GDC guidance on ethical advertising
citation.section: Dentists Act 1984 Part III/IV; GDC Standards Principle 1 (put patients' interests first) and ethical advertising guidance

applies_when: operates a dental practice or dental service in the UK
advertises dental treatments, prices or team credentials
excluded_when: not a dental service

website_obligations.duty: Make dental advertising clear, accurate and not misleading, and display the GDC registration numbers of the registered team
elements: GDC registration number(s) displayed for registered dentists and DCPs
practice owner and complaints procedure identifiable
no misleading or unverifiable comparative claims
price/finance information clear and not misleading

penalty.basis: Enforced by the GDC via fitness-to-practise proceedings under the Dentists Act 1984 (sanctions up to erasure); misuse of protected dental titles is a separate criminal offence

intel.why_matters: Dental marketing is one of the most aggressive in healthcare, and the GDC treats misleading claims and hidden registration details as fitness-to-practise matters.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are the GDC registration numbers shown and are treatment and price claims accurate and not misleading?
intel.relevance_hook: A dental site that hides its GDC numbers or over-promises 'perfect smiles' invites both patient distrust and a GDC complaint; showing registration is a quick credibility win.

records.name: Dentists Act 1984 (restriction on use of protected dental specialist titles)

citation.section: s.38 (restriction on use of titles implying registration/specialist status); GDC specialist lists

applies_when: website uses a protected specialist title (e.g. orthodontist, oral surgeon, endodontist, periodontist)
implies a clinician is a registered dental specialist
excluded_when: NEGATION GUARD: describing a 'special interest' in a field, clearly distinguished from a protected specialist title, is not a breach
the named clinician is actually on the relevant GDC specialist list

website_obligations.duty: Only describe a clinician with a protected dental specialist title if that clinician is entered on the corresponding GDC specialist list
elements: any specialist title matches a name on the GDC specialist list
'special interest' wording used where the clinician is not a listed specialist

penalty.basis: Using a protected dental title without entitlement is a criminal offence under the Dentists Act 1984 punishable by a fine (unlimited on conviction in England), alongside GDC action

intel.why_matters: Calling a dentist a 'specialist orthodontist' when they are not on the GDC specialist list is a criminal misrepresentation, not marketing licence.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does every specialist title on the site match a name on the GDC specialist list?
intel.relevance_hook: Specialist titles convert patients, which is exactly why misusing them is policed; the safe wording is 'dentist with a special interest' unless the clinician is actually listed.

records.name: GPhC registration and MHRA distance-selling requirements for online pharmacies

citation.act: Pharmacy Order 2010 (GPhC statutory basis); The Human Medicines Regulations 2012 (SI 2012/1916) distance-selling provisions; MHRA distance-selling logo scheme
citation.section: HMR 2012 Part 12 (sale/supply and registered pharmacies); GPhC standards for registered pharmacies; MHRA distance-selling logo register

applies_when: sells or supplies medicines to the public online (distance selling / internet pharmacy)
operates as a GPhC-registered pharmacy offering services online
excluded_when: no medicines sold or supplied online
provider is not a registered pharmacy and makes no pharmacy claim

website_obligations.duty: Display GPhC registration details and, where selling medicines at a distance, register with and display the MHRA distance-selling logo linking to the MHRA medicine-seller register
elements: GPhC registration number and registered pharmacy identity shown
superintendent/responsible pharmacist identifiable
MHRA distance-selling logo displayed on pages offering medicines, linking to the MHRA register
the pharmacy appears on the MHRA medicine-seller register

website_obligations.duty: Meet GPhC online safeguards for prescribing (no inappropriate online supply of high-risk or POM medicines without proper checks)
elements: prescribing safeguards described
identity and clinical checks referenced for POM supply

penalty.basis: GPhC can impose conditions, suspend or remove registration; unlawful online supply of medicines is a criminal offence under HMR 2012 with unlimited fines; the GPhC internet-pharmacy logo scheme itself is voluntary

intel.why_matters: Online pharmacies are a top enforcement target; a missing MHRA distance-selling logo or absent register listing signals an illegitimate seller to both regulators and patients.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is the pharmacy on the MHRA medicine-seller register, is the distance-selling logo shown and linked, and are GPhC registration details displayed?
intel.relevance_hook: The distance-selling logo and register link are the credibility marks buyers and regulators look for; their absence is the first thing that separates a legitimate online pharmacy from a rogue one.

sources: seed compliance_laws.json NEON-UK-GPHC (gap-filled with statutory and register detail)

records.name: CAP Code Section 12 (medicines, medical devices, health and beauty products and therapies)

citation.act: UK Code of Non-broadcast Advertising and Direct & Promotional Marketing (CAP Code)
citation.section: Section 12 (medicines, medical devices, health-related products and beauty products)

applies_when: makes health, beauty or treatment claims in marketing communications to UK consumers
excluded_when: no health, medical or beauty claims made

website_obligations.duty: Ensure health and beauty claims are accurate, substantiated and not misleading, with only suitably qualified people offering treatment or advice
elements: objective claims backed by evidence held before publishing
no discouraging essential medical treatment
testimonials genuine and not used to make unproven efficacy claims
weight-loss and serious-condition claims meet the stricter Section 12 rules

penalty.basis: Non-statutory self-regulatory code; sanctions are published upheld rulings, ad removal, paid-search denial and, for persistent breaches, referral to Trading Standards or the MHRA who hold statutory powers

regulator.name: Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) / Committee of Advertising Practice (CAP)

intel.why_matters: Health and beauty claims are the most-complained-about advertising category, and an upheld ASA ruling is a public record that follows the clinic's name in search.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Can every health or beauty claim on the site be substantiated with evidence held before it was published?
intel.relevance_hook: Unsubstantiated 'cures', dramatic before/afters and unqualified advice are exactly what the ASA publishes rulings on; tightening claims protects both rankings and reputation.

records.name: CAP Code rule 12.12 and cosmetic interventions guidance (marketing of cosmetic procedures)

citation.act: UK Code of Non-broadcast Advertising and Direct & Promotional Marketing (CAP Code)
citation.section: rule 12.12 and CAP guidance on advertising cosmetic interventions (in force October 2021)

applies_when: markets surgical or non-surgical cosmetic interventions to UK consumers
excluded_when: not a cosmetic-intervention service

website_obligations.duty: Advertise cosmetic interventions responsibly: do not trivialise, do not pressure, do not target under-18s, and do not imply a normal appearance is inadequate
elements: no trivialisation of surgical or invasive procedures
before/after images do not create unrealistic expectations
no promotions or imagery targeting under-18s
no use of under-18-appealing influencers
no body-image pressure suggesting normal features need fixing

penalty.basis: Non-statutory CAP Code rule; sanctions are published rulings, ad bans and referral onward; overlaps with MHRA (if a POM is named) and the Botulinum Toxin and Cosmetic Fillers (Children) Act 2021 where minors are involved

regulator.name: Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) / Committee of Advertising Practice (CAP)

intel.why_matters: Cosmetic-intervention marketing is held to a stricter standard precisely because it targets appearance anxiety; the 2021 rules ban the exact tactics most aesthetics sites use.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Do the ads avoid trivialising procedures, pressuring viewers and appealing to under-18s?
intel.relevance_hook: Discount-driven, body-shaming or youth-facing cosmetic ads are the pattern the ASA rules against; compliant, realistic messaging both satisfies the code and reads as more professional.

records.name: Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act 1990 and HFEA Code of Practice (fertility success-rate and add-on advertising)

citation.act: Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act 1990 (as amended); HFEA Code of Practice (issued under s.25) and licence conditions
citation.section: HFE Act 1990 s.25 (Code of Practice); Code of Practice guidance on information for patients, success rates and treatment add-ons

applies_when: an HFEA-licensed clinic advertises IVF or other licensed treatment success rates, prices or treatment add-ons
excluded_when: provider is not carrying on HFEA-licensed activity
NEGATION GUARD: linking to the HFEA verified success-rate data and traffic-light add-on ratings is COMPLIANT, not a breach

website_obligations.duty: Present success rates and treatment add-ons clearly and without misleading patients, using HFEA-verified data and the HFEA add-on rating where add-ons are offered
elements: success rates presented on a comparable, non-misleading basis (per cycle, age-banded)
treatment add-ons state the evidence of effectiveness, risks and cost
link to the HFEA add-on traffic-light ratings where add-ons are promoted
clinic's HFEA licence status shown

penalty.basis: Breach of Code guidance is a breach of licence conditions; the HFEA can vary, suspend or revoke a clinic's licence; misleading claims can also engage ASA and CMA/consumer-law action

intel.why_matters: Fertility patients are emotionally vulnerable and add-ons are a known mis-selling risk; the HFEA now expects verified success rates and honest add-on ratings on clinic sites.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are success rates shown on a comparable, non-misleading basis and do add-ons carry the HFEA evidence rating, cost and risks?
intel.relevance_hook: Inflated success rates and unrated add-ons are the exact issues the HFEA and CMA have targeted; presenting verified data with the add-on rating is both compliant and more persuasive to informed patients.

records.name: Consumer law on care home fees and terms (CMA consumer-protection guidance for care homes)

citation.act: Consumer Rights Act 2015; Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading Regulations 2008 / Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 Part 4; CMA care homes consumer-law advice
citation.section: CRA 2015 Part 2 (unfair terms) and Part 1 (information); DMCC Act 2024 Part 4 (unfair commercial practices); CMA 'Care homes: consumer law advice for providers'

applies_when: markets residential or nursing care home places to self-funding residents or their families
excluded_when: not a care home / no self-funder contracting

website_obligations.duty: Give clear, upfront and fair information about fees and contract terms before a resident commits, and avoid unfair terms
elements: weekly/monthly fees and what they include stated up front
additional/one-off charges disclosed clearly
policy on fee increases and on charges after a resident moves out or dies stated fairly
no hidden or unfair large upfront fees or open-ended post-death charging
complaints and cancellation rights explained

penalty.basis: Consumer-law enforcement by the CMA and Trading Standards; under the DMCC Act 2024 the CMA can impose direct penalties up to 10% of global turnover for unfair commercial practices; unfair contract terms are unenforceable under the CRA 2015

regulator.name: Competition and Markets Authority (CMA); local authority Trading Standards

intel.why_matters: Care home fee transparency has been a CMA priority since its 2018 market study; hidden fees, unfair upfront charges and charging after death are the practices it acts on.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are fees, what they include and the policy on increases and post-departure charges shown clearly and fairly before a family commits?
intel.relevance_hook: Families compare care homes on cost and fairness; publishing clear, fair fee information both meets consumer law and wins trust at the most anxious decision a family makes.

records.name: NHS identity and consumer law (private providers must not imply NHS affiliation)

citation.act: NHS identity guidelines (DHSC/NHS England); Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading Regulations 2008 / Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 Part 4 (misleading practices); trade mark protection of the NHS name and logo
citation.section: DMCC Act 2024 Part 4 (misleading actions); NHS identity guidelines on use of the NHS logo and name

applies_when: a private provider references the NHS, uses NHS branding, or could imply NHS provision or endorsement
excluded_when: NEGATION GUARD: an accurate statement of a genuine NHS contract or clearly labelled NHS/private split is COMPLIANT
provider holds authorised rights to use the NHS identity

website_obligations.duty: Do not use the NHS logo, name or look-and-feel in a way that falsely implies NHS provision or endorsement; distinguish private services clearly
elements: NHS logo used only where authorised
no implication of NHS endorsement or that private care is NHS-funded
private and any genuine NHS services clearly distinguished

penalty.basis: Misleadingly implying NHS status is an unfair commercial practice (CMA/Trading Standards enforcement, DMCC Act 2024 penalties up to 10% of global turnover) and can infringe the protected NHS trade mark

regulator.name: NHS England (identity); Competition and Markets Authority / Trading Standards (consumer law)

intel.why_matters: 'NHS' is a trust shortcut, so private providers borrowing NHS branding to look official is both a trade-mark and a consumer-protection problem.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does the site imply NHS provision or endorsement it is not entitled to claim?
intel.relevance_hook: Blue NHS-style badges or 'NHS' wording on a purely private site mislead patients about who they are dealing with; clean separation protects the provider and the patient.

records.name: Veterinary Medicines Regulations 2013 (restriction on advertising POM-V veterinary medicines to the public)

citation.section: Schedule 1 (advertising of veterinary medicinal products); restriction on advertising POM-V to persons other than vets/pharmacists

applies_when: a veterinary practice or retailer advertises or sells veterinary medicines online
content names or promotes a POM-V product to the public
excluded_when: advertising directed only at vets/pharmacists via a professional channel
product is an authorised over-the-counter (e.g. AVM-GSL/NFA-VPS) category advertised per its authorisation

website_obligations.duty: Do not advertise POM-V veterinary medicines to the public; restrict such promotion to professionals and follow the authorised information for products that may be advertised
elements: no public-facing promotion of POM-V products
OTC veterinary products advertised in line with their authorisation
seller of veterinary medicines is registered where required

penalty.basis: Breach is a criminal offence under the Veterinary Medicines Regulations 2013 enforced by the VMD, with unlimited fines and up to two years' imprisonment on conviction

intel.why_matters: Vet practices increasingly sell medicines online, and POM-V products carry the same public-advertising ban as human POMs.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does any public page promote a POM-V veterinary medicine rather than restricting it to professionals?
intel.relevance_hook: An online vet shop that lists POM-V products in public view is breaching the same kind of rule as a clinic naming Botox; the fix is gating or removing POM-V promotion.

records.name: RCVS Code of Professional Conduct (honest advertising and title use by veterinary practices)

citation.act: Veterinary Surgeons Act 1966 (regulator's statutory basis); RCVS Code of Professional Conduct for Veterinary Surgeons
citation.section: Veterinary Surgeons Act 1966 (protected titles, register); RCVS Code, guidance on advertising and communication

applies_when: operates a veterinary practice in the UK
advertises veterinary services or clinician credentials
excluded_when: not a veterinary service

website_obligations.duty: Keep veterinary advertising honest and not misleading, avoid unsubstantiated superiority claims, and use protected titles only where entitled
elements: no misleading or inaccurate service claims
no unsubstantiated 'best'/superiority claims
'veterinary surgeon' and RCVS registration used accurately
RCVS-registered status identifiable

penalty.basis: Enforced by the RCVS via disciplinary proceedings under the Veterinary Surgeons Act 1966 (up to removal from the register); misuse of the protected 'veterinary surgeon' title is a criminal offence

intel.why_matters: Veterinary marketing is trusted like medical marketing, and the RCVS holds practices to honesty and accurate title-use standards.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are service and superiority claims accurate and substantiated, and is the protected 'veterinary surgeon' title used correctly?
intel.relevance_hook: 'The best vet in the county' with no basis is exactly the kind of claim the RCVS treats as misleading; accurate, evidenced messaging protects both registration and client trust.

records.name: Opticians Act 1989 (protected optical titles, GOC registration and sale of optical appliances)

citation.section: s.28 (restrictions on use of titles), s.24-27 (registration and restrictions on testing sight and selling optical appliances)

applies_when: provides sight testing, dispenses spectacles/contact lenses, or uses protected optical titles (optometrist, ophthalmic optician, dispensing optician)
sells optical appliances online where a registrant's involvement is required
excluded_when: the named practitioner is on the GOC register
sale of ready-readers/plano appliances outside the restricted categories

website_obligations.duty: Use protected optical titles only for GOC-registered practitioners and make GOC registration identifiable
elements: protected titles used only by GOC-registered individuals
GOC registration identifiable for the practice/practitioners
online supply of prescription optical appliances follows the registrant/verification requirements

penalty.basis: Using a protected optical title without registration, or breaching the restrictions on sight testing/appliance sale, is a criminal offence under the Opticians Act 1989 (fine on conviction), alongside GOC action

intel.why_matters: 'Optometrist' and related titles are legally protected, and online sale of prescription eyewear is restricted; misuse is a criminal offence, not a branding choice.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are protected optical titles used only by GOC-registered practitioners, and is registration identifiable?
intel.relevance_hook: An optical site that labels staff 'optometrists' who are not GOC-registered, or sells prescription eyewear without the required checks, is breaching the Opticians Act; showing GOC registration is the fix.

records.name: Health and Social Work Professions Order 2001 (HCPC protected titles, e.g. physiotherapist)

citation.act: The Health Professions Order 2001 (SI 2002/254), as amended (HCPC statutory basis)
citation.section: art 39 (offences relating to protected titles); HCPC register of protected titles including 'physiotherapist'

applies_when: uses an HCPC-protected title (physiotherapist, and other regulated AHP/psychology titles)
advertises clinicians under a protected title
excluded_when: NEGATION GUARD: unprotected descriptors (e.g. 'sports therapist', 'massage therapist') used honestly where the person is not HCPC-registered are not a breach
the named clinician is on the HCPC register

website_obligations.duty: Use HCPC-protected titles only for currently HCPC-registered practitioners and make registration identifiable
elements: protected titles (e.g. physiotherapist) used only by HCPC-registered individuals
HCPC registration number identifiable for named clinicians
unprotected descriptors used where the practitioner is not registered

penalty.basis: Using an HCPC-protected title without registration, or falsely implying registration, is a criminal offence under art 39 of the 2001 Order (fine on conviction)

intel.why_matters: 'Physiotherapist' is a legally protected title; clinics that apply it to unregistered staff commit a criminal offence and mislead patients about qualifications.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is every use of 'physiotherapist' or another protected title backed by a current HCPC registration?
intel.relevance_hook: Patients equate the title with regulation; showing HCPC numbers proves it, while calling an unregistered practitioner a 'physiotherapist' is both illegal and a trust risk.
